title: Corona Heights Park may be San Francisco’s best-kept secret
content: # This hilltop park may be San Francisco’s best-kept secret. Kids play at States Street Playground at San Francisco’s Corona Heights Park, one of the city’s overlooked outdoor spaces. Corona Heights Park in San Francisco on Wednesday, July 31, 2024. Thirty-five years and many political negotiations later, the cursed land turned into Corona Heights Park, perhaps the best-kept secret in San Francisco’s sprawling park system. There’s a clear hero in its formation: longtime parks superintendent Josephine Randall, who did more for the children of San Francisco than almost anyone in the city’s history. Only Josephine Randall had a vision for Corona Heights, as “a spot in the heart of the city where young people could spend a day in the country.” The park lover and hero of this story, Randall became the city’s superintendent of recreation in 1926, and spent most of the next 25 years overshadowed by legendary Golden Gate Park Superintendent John McLaren — while quietly getting things done for the children of the city.
